notes: EFF Legal Director since before 2024, specializing in AI policy, copyright, and free speech. Testified before Congress on Section 230 and DMCA issues. Co-signed Mozilla's joint statement on AI safety and openness. Consistently argues that context matters in AI regulation and warns against rushing to broad regulatory solutions that could harm beneficial uses.

name: Ben Wizner
notes: Has been Edward Snowden's attorney and gained prominence in surveillance law. In 2017, wrote extensively about AI surveillance threats in partnership with AI Now initiative. Warns that automated surveillance systems threaten constitutional rights and could create a society where people feel watched 'all the time.' Focuses primarily on civil liberties implications rather than technical AI safety.

name: Janet Haven
notes: Chaired the Rights, Trust, and Safety working group of the National AI Advisory Committee from 2022-2025. Previously spent over a decade at Open Society Foundations overseeing technology and human rights grantmaking. Co-authored op-eds criticizing industry self-regulation and advocating for stronger government oversight of AI systems. Focuses extensively on sociotechnical research approaches and worker participation in AI governance.

name: danah boyd
notes: Founded Data & Society in 2013 to study sociotechnical systems and bias. Recently published 'We Need an Interventionist Mindset' (March 2025) arguing against deterministic AI rhetoric. Elected AAAS fellow in 2025. Currently conducting ethnographic study of US Census to understand data legitimacy. Known for critiquing both AI hype and fear cycles, advocating for probabilistic rather than deterministic thinking about AI futures.

name: Alexandra Reeve Givens
notes: Testified before House Education and Workforce Committee in January 2026 on 'Building an AI-Ready America.' Criticized narrow focus on long-term risks at 2023 Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit, advocating for attention to current AI harms. Daughter of actor Christopher Reeve, serves on foundation board focused on spinal cord injury research using AI technologies.

name: Sarah Myers West
notes: Served as Senior Advisor on AI at the Federal Trade Commission where she worked on competition and consumer protection matters. Testified before Congress on AI, competition and data privacy issues in December 2023. Co-authored influential AI Now Institute reports on AI governance and delivered keynote at Paris AI Action Summit in February 2025 focusing on AI's environmental impacts. Has forthcoming book 'Tracing Code' (University of California Press) examining origins of data capitalism.

name: Safiya Noble
notes: Noble's 2018 book 'Algorithms of Oppression' documented how Google search results for 'Black girls' returned pornographic content, demonstrating algorithmic bias in search engines. She was awarded a MacArthur Fellowship in 2021 for her groundbreaking work on algorithmic discrimination. Her research focuses on how AI systems reinforce racism and sexism, particularly affecting marginalized communities, and she argues that AI will become a major human rights issue in the 21st century.

name: Ruha Benjamin
notes: Coined the term 'New Jim Code' to describe how digital technologies embed racism while appearing neutral. Her 2019 book 'Race After Technology' won the Oliver Cromwell Cox Book Award for anti-racist scholarship. Delivered 2024 Tanner Lectures at Harvard arguing tech elites cannot be trusted as stewards of collective good. Founded the Ida B. Wells Just Data Lab focusing on algorithmic justice.

name: Cathy O'Neil
notes: Founded ORCAA in 2017 to audit algorithms for bias and fairness. Her 2016 book 'Weapons of Math Destruction' was longlisted for the National Book Award and became a NYT bestseller. Testified to Congress on algorithmic accountability and developed the 'Ethical Matrix' framework for auditing AI systems. Won the MAA Euler Book Prize in 2019.

name: Virginia Eubanks
notes: Eubanks' 2018 book 'Automating Inequality' investigates how AI systems in welfare, housing, and child welfare disproportionately harm poor and working-class people. She coined the term 'digital poorhouse' to describe technological systems that embed assumptions about poverty. Her work focuses on algorithmic accountability and has won multiple awards including the 2019 Lillian Smith Book Award and 2018 McGannon Center Book Prize.

name: Lina Khan
notes: Led FTC's AI enforcement including OpenAI investigation (July 2023), Rite Aid AI facial recognition ban (December 2023), and joint interagency AI statement with DOJ/CFPB/EEOC (April 2023). Published op-ed 'We Must Regulate A.I.' in New York Times. Estimated 15% p(doom) according to sources. Known for aggressive antitrust enforcement and authored influential 'Amazon's Antitrust Paradox' as law student.

name: Alan Davidson
notes: Led NTIA's AI Accountability Policy Report (March 2024) calling for improved transparency, independent evaluations, and consequences for AI systems imposing unacceptable risks. Currently working on assessment of benefits and harms of open-weight AI models per Biden's AI Executive Order, with report due to White House in July 2024. Co-leads President's Task Force on Kids Online Health and Safety and oversees $42 billion broadband infrastructure programs.

name: Laurie Locascio
notes: As NIST Director (2022-2024), Locascio launched the U.S. AI Safety Institute in November 2023 and oversaw release of the AI Risk Management Framework in January 2023. She participated in roundtables on AI safety and testified before Congress on NIST's AI priorities, securing $50M in FY2025 budget for AI safety work. Now leads ANSI, coordinating private sector standards development including AI standards.

name: Elham Tabassi
notes: Iranian-born, joined NIST in 1999 after coming to US in 1994 for master's in electrical engineering. Principal architect of NIST Fingerprint Image Quality standard used by FBI. Led development of AI Risk Management Framework 1.0 released January 2023 that laid groundwork for Biden's AI Executive Order. Named by Time Magazine as one of 100 most influential people in AI in 2024.

name: Jonathan Kanter
notes: Led DOJ Antitrust Division 2021-2024, confirmed by Senate 68-29. Delivered remarks at Stanford AI workshop stating DOJ would scrutinize AI sector 'with urgency' to prevent monopoly consolidation. Worked with FTC to divide AI oversight responsibilities - DOJ handles chip manufacturers while FTC oversees software companies. Appointed DOJ's first Chief Technologist to enhance AI expertise for investigations.

name: Jen Easterly
notes: Served as CISA Director from July 2021 to January 2025. While CISA's mandate is primarily cybersecurity, the agency had a supporting role in implementing parts of Biden's Executive Order 14110 on AI safety (Oct 2023, since rescinded by Trump's EO 14179 in Jan 2025). Frequently warns that AI represents 'the most powerful weapon of our time' and advocates for 'secure-by-design' AI development. Named CEO of RSA Conference in January 2026 after leaving CISA. Rhodes Scholar and 20-year Army veteran who helped establish US Cyber Command.

name: Stefano Mazzocchi
notes: No information found in search results for a Stefano Mazzocchi serving as Chief AI Officer at the Department of Commerce. Search results show other individuals with similar names in different roles.

name: Margrethe Vestager
notes: Led development of the EU AI Act (2021-2024) establishing world's first major AI legislation with risk-based approach. Known as aggressive antitrust enforcer against Big Tech companies including landmark cases against Google, Apple, Amazon, and Facebook. Served as EU Competition Commissioner 2014-2024 and Executive Vice President for Digital Age 2019-2024. Described by critics as implementing 'anti-American antitrust' policies that disproportionately target U.S. tech companies.

name: Thierry Breton
notes: Led the 38-hour 'trilogue' negotiations that finalized the EU AI Act in December 2023. Sanctioned by the US in December 2024 with travel ban and asset freeze for his role in EU tech regulation, specifically the Digital Services Act. Previously wrote prescient sci-fi novels in the 1980s about fake news and generative AI. Resigned from the European Commission in September 2024 amid tensions with Ursula von der Leyen.

name: Ted Chiang
notes: Won four Hugo Awards, four Nebula Awards, and 2024 PEN/Malamud Award for short story excellence. Named one of Time's 100 Most Influential People in AI (2023). Wrote influential New Yorker essays including "Why A.I. Isn't Going to Make Art" and "ChatGPT Is a Blurry JPEG of the Web" arguing AI cannot create true art due to lack of intentional choice-making. Studied computer science at Brown University, providing technical foundation for his AI commentary.

name: Yuval Noah Harari
notes: Delivered major AI warnings at Davos 2026 WEF, predicting AI will create identity and immigration crises for every country. Warns that 'anything made of words will be taken over by AI' including laws, books, and religions. Has sold over 50 million books in 65 languages and regularly advises world leaders including Emmanuel Macron and Angela Merkel. Published 'Nexus: A Brief History of Information Networks from the Stone Age to AI' in 2024.

name: Kate Crawford
notes: Co-authored influential 2018 paper 'Datasheets for Datasets' with Timnit Gebru that has been integrated into the EU AI Act. Her work 'Anatomy of an AI System' with Vladan Joler won the Beazley Design Award 2019 and is in MoMA's permanent collection. Testified before U.S. Senate Committee on the Judiciary in 2023 arguing that AI half-measures like transparency and bias audits are insufficient. Co-founded AI Now Institute in 2017 to explore policy approaches to AI's social consequences.

name: Cory Doctorow
notes: Coined the viral term 'enshittification' to describe platform decay. Has upcoming book 'The Reverse Centaur's Guide to Life After AI' (Farrar, Straus and Giroux / Verso, June 23 2026) — title invokes the 'reverse centaur' figure forced by technology to work at an inhuman pace. Views AI as 'a collection of moderately useful tools being billed as a supernatural being' and argues the AI bubble will cause economic damage when it deflates. Warns that 'AI can't do your job, but an AI salesman can convince your boss to fire you and replace you with AI that can't do your job.'

name: Naomi Klein
notes: Published 2023 Guardian piece 'AI machines aren't hallucinating, But their makers are' arguing AI development represents 'the largest theft in human history.' Describes AI as creating a 'three-legged stool' of tech, fossil fuels, and state power that provides a lifeline to fossil fuel industries. Warns AI undermines belief in reality itself through deepfakes and generated content.

name: Brian Christian
notes: Won 2022 National Academies Eric and Wendy Schmidt Awards for Excellence in Science Communications. Currently pursuing DPhil at Oxford under Chris Summerfield focusing on cognitive models of human decision-making for AI alignment. His book 'The Alignment Problem' was called 'the one book to read on AI' by The New York Times and bridges technical AI safety with present-day algorithmic bias issues.

name: Shalini Kantayya
notes: Her 2020 Sundance documentary 'Coded Bias' follows MIT researcher Joy Buolamwini's discovery of racial bias in facial recognition systems and advocates for algorithmic justice. The film was broadcast on PBS Independent Lens and globally on Netflix, winning Best Director at Social Impact Media Awards. Her work focuses on how AI systems perpetuate inequality and erode civil rights, particularly impacting marginalized communities through biased hiring, healthcare, and criminal justice algorithms.

name: Stanford Digital Economy Lab
notes: Stanford Digital Economy Lab, directed by Erik Brynjolfsson, researches the economic effects of AI on labor, productivity, and inequality. In Dec 2025 it published The Digitalist Papers Vol. 2 on transformative AI economics. Research shows early evidence of AI displacing entry-level workers, informing targeted labor and technology policy recommendations.

name: Stanford SIEPR
notes: Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research (SIEPR) is Stanford''s flagship economic-policy center. While not AI-specific, it hosts significant AI-and-labor and AI-and-productivity research (including Erik Brynjolfsson''s Digital Economy Lab) and serves as a venue for economists engaging AI''s macroeconomic and workforce implications.
